reason: The text does not contain any statement about a Hebrew leader loving the people. While the passage narrates the birth of Moses (who would become a Hebrew leader), he is depicted as an infant and not yet in a leadership role. Joseph, the prominent Hebrew figure of the prior generation, has already died at the beginning of this passage. The text focuses instead on Pharaoh's fear of the multiplying Israelites and his ensuing oppression of them, along with acts of compassion shown by the Egyptian midwives and Pharaoh's daughter toward Hebrew children. No Hebrew leader expresses or demonstrates love for the people in this passage.
